hints: There is is used with singular nouns. After the gap, 'a book' is a single item.
The article 'a' before 'book' signals singular — use there is.

a1.There is: Correct: 'a book' is singular, so we use there is.
a1.There are: Wrong: there are is for plurals. 'A book' is singular.
a1.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense. The sentence describes a present state.

hints: There are is used with plural nouns. 'Two cats' means more than one.
The number 'two' clearly indicates plural — use there are.

a2.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular. 'Two cats' is plural.
a2.There are: Correct: 'two cats' is plural, so we use there are.
a2.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A lamp' is a single item — article 'a' signals singular. Use there is.
After the gap, 'a lamp in the room' — one object.

a3.There is: Correct: 'a lamp' is singular, so there is.
a3.There are: Wrong: there are is for plurals.
a3.There were: Wrong: there were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Three chairs' is plural. The number 'three' signals multiple items.
Use there are with plural nouns.

a4.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular. 'Three chairs' is plural.
a4.There are: Correct: 'three chairs' is plural, so there are.
a4.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A dog' — one animal. Article 'a' signals singular.
Use there is with a singular noun.

a5.There is: Correct: 'a dog' is singular, so there is.
a5.There are: Wrong: there are is for plurals.
a5.There were: Wrong: there were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Five apples' is plural. The number 'five' means multiple items.
Use there are for plural nouns.

a6.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular.
a6.There are: Correct: 'five apples' is plural, so there are.
a6.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A pen' — one item. Article 'a' = singular.
Use there is for one item.

a7.There is: Correct: 'a pen' is singular, so there is.
a7.There are: Wrong: there are requires plural nouns.
a7.There were: Wrong: there were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Many toys' — plural. 'Many' always means more than one.
After 'many', always use there are.

a8.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular. 'Many toys' is plural.
a8.There are: Correct: 'many toys' is plural, so there are.
a8.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A window' — one window. Article 'a' = singular.
Use there is for a single item.

a9.There are: Wrong: there are is for plural nouns.
a9.There were: Wrong: there were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Four cups' — plural. Number 'four' signals multiple items.
Use there are with plural nouns.

a10.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular.
a10.There are: Correct: 'four cups' is plural, so there are.
a10.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A clock' — one item. Article 'a' = singular.
Use there is for one object.

a11.There are: Wrong: there are is for plurals.
a11.There were: Wrong: there were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Some flowers' — plural. 'Flowers' has -s ending = plural.
Use there are for multiple items.

a12.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular.
a12.There are: Correct: 'some flowers' is plural, so there are.
a12.There was: Wrong: there was is past tense singular.

hints: After 'There', choose the correct form of be. 'A ball' = singular → is.
Article 'a' before 'ball' = one item.

a13.is: Correct: 'a ball' is singular → There is.
a13.are: Wrong: are is for plural. One ball.

hints: 'Two birds' = plural. After There, use are.
Number 'two' = plural.

a14.is: Wrong: is is for singular. 'Two birds' is plural.

hints: 'A phone' = one item. Article 'a' = singular → is.
After 'There', singular nouns take is.
'Ten students' = plural. Number 'ten' signals many.
Use are for plural nouns after There.

a16.is: Wrong: is is for singular. 'Ten students' is plural.

hints: 'A car' = one vehicle. Article 'a' = singular.
Use There is for a single item.

a17.There is: Correct: 'a car' is singular → There is.
a17.There are: Wrong: There are is for plurals.

hints: 'Seven days' = plural. A well-known fact.
Number 'seven' signals plural → There are.

a18.There is: Wrong: there is is for singular.
a18.There are: Correct: 'seven days' is plural → There are.

hints: 'A picture' = one item. Article 'a' = singular → is.
After 'There', one picture → is.

a19.were: Wrong: were is past tense plural.

hints: 'Six eggs' = plural. Number 'six' = multiple items → are.
Use are for plural nouns.

a20.was: Wrong: was is past tense singular.

hints: 'A mirror' = one item. Article 'a' = singular.
Use There is for a single item.

a21.There are: Wrong: There are is for plurals.

hints: 'Three books' = plural. Number 'three' = multiple.
Use There are for plural nouns.
'A hat' = one item. Article 'a' = singular → is.
After 'There', one hat → is.
'Many stars' = plural. 'Many' = more than one → are.
After 'many', use are.
'Milk' is uncountable. Use is (not) with uncountable nouns.
Uncountable nouns always take singular verb forms.

a25.are: Wrong: are is for countable plurals.

hints: 'Chairs' = plural countable noun → are.
The -s ending on 'chairs' shows it's plural.
In questions, there is becomes Is there...? 'A hospital' = singular → Is.
Article 'a' before 'hospital' = singular.

a27.Is: Correct: 'a hospital' is singular → Is there...?

hints: 'Any shops' = plural. Question form: Are there...?
The noun 'shops' has -s = plural → Are.
'Water' is uncountable → is.
Uncountable nouns take singular verb forms.
'Some children' = plural (children is irregular plural of child).
'Children' is plural → are.
'Sugar' is uncountable. Question: Is there any sugar...?
Uncountable nouns → Is.
'Any buses' = plural. Question: Are there any buses...?
'Buses' has -es ending = plural → Are.
'Bread' is uncountable → is no bread.
Uncountable nouns take singular forms.
'No windows' = plural → are.
'Windows' has -s ending = plural.
'A pharmacy' = singular. Question: Is there...?
Article 'a' = singular.

a35.Is: Correct: 'a pharmacy' is singular → Is there...?

hints: 'Enough chairs' = plural → are.
'Chairs' is plural → are not enough.

a36.are: Correct: 'chairs' is plural → are not enough.

hints: 'Juice' is uncountable → is.
Uncountable nouns take singular forms.
'Any museums' = plural → Are there...?
'Museums' has -s = plural.
'Money' is uncountable → is no money.
'Money' doesn't have a plural form in English.
'Some new students' = plural → are.
'Students' has -s = plural.
'A library' = singular. Question: Is there...?
Article 'a' = singular → Is.

a41.Is: Correct: 'a library' is singular → Is there...?

hints: 'No parks' = plural → are no.
'Parks' has -s = plural.
'Rice' is uncountable → is.
Uncountable nouns take singular forms.
'Any good restaurants' = plural → Are there...?
'Restaurants' has -s = plural.
'Homework' is uncountable → is not.
'Homework' is always singular in English.

a45.is: Correct: 'homework' is uncountable → is not any.

hints: 'Some oranges' = plural → are.
'Oranges' has -s = plural.
'A post office' = singular → Is there...?
Article 'a' = singular.
'No clean towels' = plural → are no.
'Towels' has -s = plural.
Past tense of there is → there was. 'A big park' = singular past.
'When I was a child' indicates past tense → was.

a49.were: Wrong: were is for plural past.

hints: 'Many people' = plural in past tense → were.
'Last night' = past tense. 'Many people' = plural → were.
Past question with plural: Were there...?
'Any problems' = plural. 'Yesterday' = past → Were.
'Electricity' is uncountable, past tense → was.
'Last week' = past. 'Electricity' = uncountable → was.
There is going to be = future with singular. 'A meeting' = singular → is.
'Tomorrow' = future. 'A meeting' = singular → is going to be.

a53.is: Correct: singular → is going to be.

hints: 'Several accidents' = plural, past → were.
'Last month' = past. 'Several accidents' = plural → were.
There will be = future tense with modal 'will'.
'Next year' = future → will be.

a55.will: Correct: there will be — future construction.

hints: 'A lot of noise' = uncountable, past → was.
'Last night' = past. 'Noise' = uncountable → was.
Past question with singular: Was there...?
'A cinema' = singular. 'Ten years ago' = past → Was.
'Some changes' = plural → are going to be.
'Changes' = plural → are.

a58.are: Correct: 'changes' = plural → are going to be.

hints: 'Enough time' = uncountable, past → was.
'Yesterday' = past. 'Time' = uncountable → was.
'Many tourists' = plural, past → were.
'Last summer' = past. 'Many tourists' = plural → were.
'Any letters' = plural → Were there...?
'Letters' = plural → Were.
'Any clouds' = plural, past → were.
'Yesterday' = past. 'Clouds' = plural → were not.
There must be = deduction/certainty. Modal 'must' before 'be'.
There + modal + be: must be a reason.

a63.must: Correct: there must be — modal for certainty.

hints: There might be = possibility. Modal 'might' before 'be'.
There + modal + be: might be rain.

a64.might: Correct: there might be — modal for possibility.

hints: There used to be = past habit/state. 'Used' + 'to be'.
The construction '... to be a cinema' requires 'used' to describe something that existed before.

a65.used: Correct: there used to be — describes a past state.
a65.was: Wrong: was doesn't fit before 'to be'.

hints: 'No homework' = uncountable, past → was.
'Yesterday' = past. 'Homework' = uncountable → was.
'A swimming pool' = singular. 'Old house' suggests past → Was.
Article 'a' = singular. Past context → Was there...?
'Fireworks' is always plural → are going to be.
'Fireworks' = plural noun → are.

a68.are: Correct: 'fireworks' is plural → are going to be.

hints: 'Two exams' = plural, past → were.
'Two' + 'last Friday' → plural past → were.
There should be = recommendation. Modal 'should' before 'be'.
There + modal + be: should be more parks.

a70.should: Correct: there should be — modal for recommendation.

hints: 'No water' = uncountable, past → was.
'Last summer' = past. 'Water' = uncountable → was.
There will be = future. Modal 'will' + 'be'.
There + will + be: a new bridge in the future.

a72.will: Correct: there will be — future tense.

hints: There has been = present perfect with singular/uncountable. 'Rain' = uncountable → has.
'This month' = present perfect. 'Rain' = uncountable → has.

a73.has: Correct: uncountable, present perfect → has been.

hints: 'Several complaints' = plural, present perfect → have been.
'Recently' = present perfect. 'Complaints' = plural → have.

a74.have: Correct: plural, present perfect → have been.

hints: Present perfect question with singular: Has there been...?
'Any improvement' = singular. 'Since' = present perfect → Has.

a75.Has: Correct: singular, present perfect → Has there been...?

hints: 'No progress' = uncountable, present perfect → has been.
'So far' = present perfect. 'Progress' = uncountable → has.

a76.has: Correct: uncountable, present perfect → has been no.

hints: 'Too many delays' = plural, present perfect → have been.
'This quarter' = present perfect. 'Delays' = plural → have.

a77.have: Correct: plural, present perfect → have been.

hints: 'Any updates' = plural. Question: Have there been...?
'Updates' = plural → Have.

a78.Have: Correct: plural, present perfect → Have there been...?

hints: There seems to be = hedged existence. 'A shortage' = singular → seems.
'Seems' agrees with singular 'shortage'.
There appear to be = hedged plural. 'Several options' = plural → appear.
'Appear' (no -s) agrees with plural 'options'.
'A sharp rise' = singular, present perfect → has been.
'Lately' = present perfect. 'A rise' = singular → has.

a81.has: Correct: singular, present perfect → has been.

hints: 'Some interesting developments' = plural, present perfect → have been.
'Developments' = plural → have.
There appears to be = formal hedging singular. 'A growing concern' = singular → appears.
'Appears' (with -s) agrees with singular.
'A significant drop' = singular, present perfect → has been.
'Since January' = present perfect → has.

a84.has: Correct: singular, present perfect → has been.

hints: 'Many applicants' = plural. Question: Have there been...?
'Applicants' = plural → Have.
There seems to be = hedged existence. 'A link' = singular → seems.
'Seems' agrees with singular 'link'.
'A steady increase' = singular, present perfect → has been.
'Over the past decade' = present perfect → has.
'Rumours' = plural, present perfect → have been.
'Rumours' has -s = plural → have.
There is no doubt = emphatic certainty. Fixed expression with 'is'.
'No doubt' is a set phrase with there is.

a89.is: Correct: there is no doubt — set expression.

hints: There is likely to be = probability. 'A delay' = singular → is.
Article 'a' before 'delay' = singular.

a90.is: Correct: singular → is likely to be.

hints: There are certain to be = high probability plural. 'Objections' = plural → are.
'Objections' = plural → are.

a91.are: Correct: plural → are certain to be.

hints: 'No official statement' = singular, present perfect → has been.
'Yet' = present perfect → has.
There seem to be = hedged plural. 'Fewer tourists' = plural → seem.
'Seem' (no -s) agrees with plural 'tourists'.
There happened to be = coincidence in past. 'A doctor' = singular, past → happened.
Construction: there + happened + to be describes a coincidence.

a94.happened: Correct: past coincidence → happened to be.

hints: There is bound to be = inevitable. 'Some resistance' = uncountable → is.
'Resistance' = uncountable → is bound to be.

a95.is: Correct: uncountable → is bound to be.

hints: 'Considerable debate' = uncountable, present perfect → has been.
'Debate' (uncountable sense) → has been.
There appears to have been = formal perfect hedging. 'A misunderstanding' = singular → appears.
Construction: appears + to have been — formal academic register.

a97.appears: Correct: singular → appears to have been.

hints: There would have been = third conditional (unreal past). 'If ... had acted' → would.
'If the government had acted sooner' = third conditional → would.

a98.would: Correct: third conditional → would have been.
a98.will: Wrong: will is for real future.

hints: There is no denying = it can't be denied. Set expression with 'is'.
'No denying' is an idiomatic phrase always with is.

a99.is: Correct: there is no denying — set expression.

hints: There is said to be = passive report. 'A secret passage' = singular → is.
Passive reporting construction: is said to be.

a100.is: Correct: singular → is said to be.

hints: There remain = formal existential verb. 'Several questions' = plural → remain.
'Remain' (no -s) agrees with plural 'questions'.
There exist = formal existential. 'Substantial differences' = plural → exist.
'Exist' (no -s) agrees with plural.
There seems to have been = hedged perfect. 'A significant shift' = singular → seems.
Construction: seems + to have been — formal hedging.

a103.seems: Correct: singular → seems to have been.

hints: There need not be = modal negation. Modal 'need' doesn't inflect.
Modal 'need' is invariable: there need not be.

a104.need: Correct: modal need → need not be.
a104.needs: Wrong: 'needs' is lexical verb with -s. Here modal need without -s.

hints: There are believed to be = passive report plural. 'Over a thousand species' = plural → are.
'Species' (plural: over a thousand) → are.

a105.are: Correct: plural → are believed to be.

hints: There would have been = third conditional with inversion.
'Had the speed limit been enforced' = inverted third conditional → would.

a106.would: Correct: third conditional → would have been.
a106.could: Wrong: possibility, not standard third conditional.

hints: There has emerged = formal present perfect. 'A consensus' = singular → has.
'Emerge' in present perfect with singular → has emerged.
There is no telling = impossible to say. Set expression with 'is'.
'No telling' is an idiomatic phrase always with is.

a108.is: Correct: there is no telling — set expression.

hints: There is reported to be = passive report. 'A shortage' = singular → is.
Passive report: is reported to be.

a109.is: Correct: singular → is reported to be.

hints: There has arisen = formal present perfect. 'A new challenge' = singular → has.
'Arise' (arisen) in present perfect → has arisen.
There may well be = strong possibility. Modal 'may' strengthened by 'well'.
There + may well + be — emphatic possibility.

a111.may: Correct: there may well be — emphatic possibility.
a111.might: Wrong: might well be is possible but 'may well be' is the set phrase here.

hints: There are thought to be = passive belief plural. 'Ancient ruins' = plural → are.
'Ruins' = plural → are.

a112.are: Correct: plural → are thought to be.

hints: There are known to be = passive knowledge. 'Several variants' = plural → are.
'Variants' = plural → are.

a113.are: Correct: plural → are known to be.

hints: There has proved to be = proven existence. 'A strong correlation' = singular → has.
'Prove' in present perfect → has proved to be.

a114.has: Correct: singular → has proved to be.

hints: There would have been = third conditional. 'If the warning had been issued' → would.
Third conditional structure → would have been.

a115.would: Correct: third conditional → would have been.
a115.could: Wrong: possibility, not standard third conditional.

hints: There can be no = modal impossibility. 'Can' for categorical denial.
There + can + be no — emphatic negation.

a116.can: Correct: there can be no — modal impossibility.

hints: There tends to be = habitual tendency. 'More traffic' = uncountable → tends.
'Tends' (with -s) agrees with singular/uncountable.
There were found to be = passive discovery past. 'Significant errors' = plural → were.
'Errors' = plural, past → were found to be.

a118.were: Correct: plural past → were found to be.

hints: There is alleged to be = passive allegation. 'Corruption' = uncountable → is.
'Corruption' = uncountable → is.

a119.is: Correct: uncountable → is alleged to be.

hints: There could be no better time = modal possibility. 'Could' for hypothetical.
There + could + be — hypothetical possibility.

a120.could: Correct: hypothetical → could be no better time.
a120.can: Wrong: present modal. Hypothetical needs could.
a120.may: Wrong: possibility, but the construction requires could.

hints: There comes a time = literary inversion. Verb 'come' in third person → comes.
There + comes — literary style replacing there is.

a121.comes: Correct: literary inversion, third person → comes.
a121.come: Wrong: no -s, doesn't agree with 'a time'.

hints: Were there to be = formal subjunctive conditional. After 'were there', use 'to' + infinitive.
Construction: Were there ... be — inverted conditional with 'to'.

a122.to: Correct: were there to be — formal inverted conditional.
a122.will: Wrong: will doesn't follow were in conditionals.
a122.would: Wrong: would belongs in the main clause.

hints: Seldom has there been = negative adverb inversion. 'Seldom' at start → has.
Negative adverb 'seldom' at beginning requires inversion.

a123.has: Correct: seldom has there been — negative adverb inversion.

hints: There ensued = literary past narrative. 'A heated debate' = singular past → ensued.
'Ensue' in past tense → ensued (literary narrative).
Never has there been = negative adverb inversion. 'Never' at start → has.
Negative adverb 'never' at beginning requires inversion.

a125.has: Correct: never has there been — inversion.

hints: There arose = literary past. 'A new movement' = singular past → arose.
'Arise' in past → arose (literary narrative).

a126.arisen: Wrong: past participle, not simple past.

hints: Should there be = formal subjunctive. After modal should, use base form 'be'.
Should there be = if there should be (inverted conditional).

a127.be: Correct: base form after modal should.
a127.is: Wrong: is doesn't follow modal should.
a127.are: Wrong: are doesn't follow modal should.

hints: There prevailed = literary past. 'An atmosphere' = singular past → prevailed.
'Prevail' in past → prevailed (literary narrative).
Rarely has there been = negative adverb inversion. 'Rarely' at start → has.
Negative adverb 'rarely' requires inversion.

a129.has: Correct: rarely has there been — inversion.

hints: There had existed = past perfect. 'A long-standing tradition' = singular → had.
Past perfect describes a state that lasted until a point in the past → had existed.
Had there been = inverted third conditional. After 'had there', use past participle 'been'.
Had there been more time = if there had been more time.

a131.been: Correct: past participle in third conditional.
a131.be: Wrong: base form doesn't follow had.

hints: There followed = literary past. 'A period' = singular past → followed.
'Follow' in past → followed (literary narrative).
At no point has there been = negative phrase inversion.
'At no point' at beginning requires inversion → has.
There persisted = literary past. 'A myth' = singular past → persisted.
'Persist' in past → persisted.
Not once has there been = negative phrase inversion.
'Not once' at beginning requires inversion → has.
There stood = literary positional verb. 'An ancient oak' = singular past → stood.
'Stand' in past → stood (literary description).
Were there not to be = formal negative conditional. Needs particle 'to'.
Inverted conditional: Were there not ... be → to.

a137.would: Wrong: would belongs in the main clause.

hints: There remained = literary past. 'Only a faint echo' = singular past → remained.
'Remain' in past → remained.
Little has there been = negative adverb inversion. 'Little' at start → has.
'Little' as a limiting adverb requires inversion.
There need not have been = past modal negation. Modal 'need' is invariable.
There + need not + have been — the action was unnecessary.

a140.need: Correct: modal need → need not have been.
a140.needed: Wrong: past tense of lexical verb.

hints: In no circumstances should there be = formal inversion with 'should'.
Negative phrase 'in no circumstances' requires inversion → should.

a141.should: Correct: inversion with negative phrase → should.
a141.must: Wrong: obligation, different from should in this inversion.

hints: There transpired = literary past. 'Events' = plural past → transpired.
'Transpire' in past → transpired (literary narrative).
Under no circumstances should there be = formal inversion.
Negative phrase 'under no circumstances' requires inversion → should.
There could have been = rhetorical perfect modal. 'Could' for hypothetical past.
Modal 'could' + have been — hypothetical past possibility.

a144.could: Correct: rhetorical perfect modal → could have been.
a144.can: Wrong: present modal. Past hypothetical needs could.
